chunks.title: CSCE 482 Syllabus
chunks.content:
Section 934 (37078)
Senior Capstone Design
Spring 2026 - College Station

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Senior Capstone Design. (1-6). Credit 3. Development of system integration skills for solving real-world problems in computer science; significant team software project that uses integration of advanced concepts across computer science specializations; projects require design, implementation, documentation and demonstration, as well as design methodology, management process and teamwork. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or CSCE 331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or CSCE 331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Analyze a complex computing problem and apply principles of computing and other relevant disciplines to identify solutions.
• Design, implement and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a given set of computing requirements in the context of the program's discipline.
• Communicate effectively in a variety of professional contexts.
• Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgements in computing practice based on legal and ethical principles.
• Collaborate effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities appropriate to the program's discipline.
• Apply computer science theory and software implementation fundamentals to produce computing-based solutions.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: UCRT - University Required Oral Communication

chunks.title: To receive C credit for this course...
chunks.content:
To receive C credit for this course, you must pass the C component, with the C course communication requirement below:
• A minimum of 35% of your final grade is based on written and oral communication assignments.
• Of this communication component, at least 70% is based on individually written work and individual public speaking; group work does not count toward this standard.
• To receive C credit, students must pass both the written and public speaking components of the course. Failure to pass either component will result in failing the course, regardless of performance on other assignments.
• Each student will produce at least 1250 words of individually written work and deliver a minimum of 5 minutes of individual public speaking.
• Assignments fulfilling this requirement include individual progress reports, project proposals, final technical documentation, and individual presentations or demonstrations.
• Resumes and related materials are accepted only if specific to the major; take-home essay exams may count if they allow drafting and revision. Oral presentation slides, scripts, web sites, and posters count when accompanied by individual presentations.
No more than 10% of the communication grade may be assessed by undergraduate aides, and all grading by aides is supervised by the instructor.
Failure to meet the individual written or oral communication requirements will result in failure of the course, regardless of overall numerical average.

chunks.title: ABET
chunks.content:
This course is governed by ABET (Accreditation Board for Engineering and Technology), which provides assurance that a college or university program meets the quality standards of the profession, by doing the following:
• Verifies that your educational experience meets the global standard for technical education in your profession.
• Enhances your employment opportunities—multinational corporations require graduation from an accredited program.
• Supports your entry to a technical profession through licensure, registration, and certification—all of which often require graduation from an ABET-accredited program as a minimum qualification.
• Establishes your eligibility for federal student loans, grants, and/or scholarships.
• Paves the way for you to work globally, because ABET accreditation is recognized worldwide through international agreements, and other countries' national accrediting systems are based on the ABET model.
To comply with ABET, students must demonstrate their abilities in outcomes above, which will be evaluated by external industrial affiliate members during your final presentation.

chunks.title: Required-to-Pass Capstone Completion Requirements
chunks.content:
The final grade you will receive in the class is based on points accumulated during the semester and on completion of required capstone deliverables. Thus, both continued progress (the process) and the quality of your product (and other deliverables) will determine your grade. Your grade is based on the performance of your team and your individual contribution.
This course includes non-negotiable completion requirements that supersede Canvas point totals.
To pass this course, a student must:
1. Be a contributing member of a team that delivers a working, demonstrable system that meets the agreed-upon project scope by the end of the semester.
2. Participate in the final project presentation and demonstration.
3. Submit all required final deliverables, including:
• Final project report, including all of the required components
• Final videos, poster, and presentation slides
• Final GitHub release
• Required data and materials archives
4. Pass both the written and oral communication components of the course.
5. Return all equipment
Important:
A student cannot pass this course with a numerical average alone. Failure to meet any required-to-pass condition will result in a course grade of F, regardless of Canvas calculations.
Additionally, grade will reflect your peer reviews. Since a large number of assignments are group created, your grade on those assignments will reflect your percentage effort as reflected by your peer reviews and other effort monitoring assignments. Specifically, your grade cannot be higher than the team grade on a project, but it can be lower based on your participation in the project.

chunks.title: Attendance and Professional Responsibility
chunks.content:
Capstone projects depend on consistent participation.
• Students are expected to attend class, team meetings, sponsor meetings, and scheduled presentations.
• More than five unexcused absences over the semester will result in automatic failure of the course, as excessive absence undermines team viability.
• Excused absences must follow university policy and be communicated promptly.
Professional conduct includes preparedness, respectful communication, meeting deadlines, and fulfilling agreed-upon responsibilities.

chunks.title: Teamwork and Accountability
chunks.content:
This course emphasizes collaborative, professional teamwork.
• Students will create and adhere to a Team Agreement outlining expectations, communication norms, and accountability mechanisms.
• Peer evaluations and contribution records (e.g., GitHub history) are used to assess individual participation.
• Students who fail to contribute meaningfully risk failing the course, even if the team succeeds.

chunks.title: Assignments and Evaluation
chunks.content:
The course includes (but is not limited to):
• Design exploration and planning assignments
• Iterative technical deliverables and releases
• User evaluation and functionality testing
• Written report sections with peer review
• Oral presentations and demonstrations

chunks.title: Grading Philosophy
chunks.content:
Grades reflect professional readiness, not just task completion.
• Late or missing capstone deliverables compromise team outcomes and may not be accepted.
• Canvas grade calculations are subordinate to required-to-pass conditions.
• The instructor reserves the right to assign a final grade that accurately reflects capstone completion and contribution.
Capstone is intentionally demanding. The structure of this course exists to support you in delivering a project you are proud of and that represents your readiness to enter the profession.
If you are unsure whether you are meeting expectations, ask early.

chunks.title: Grading Scale
chunks.content:
The grading scale is as follows:
F < 60% ≤ D < 70% < C < 80% ≤ B < 90% ≤ A
We do not curve in this class, however, provide you with the opportunity to earn an A, hence an 89.9 is a B. We teach proficiency, which means that we have clear expectations in terms of what you should complete and what knowledge you should have obtained by the end of the course to succeed and to earn an A.

chunks.title: The grade distribution is as follows:
chunks.content:
| Category | Percentage |
| :----------------------------------- | :--------- |
| Professional Conduct & Team Participation | 5% |
| Project Planning & Management | 10% |
| System Design & Implementation | 25% |
| Functional & User Evaluation | 20% |
| Written Technical Documentation | 20% |
| Oral Presentation & Demonstration | 15% |
| Quizzes & Short Reflections | 5% |
| **TOTAL** | **100%** |

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late work policy is defined as submission of work after the established deadline, not including work submitted late due to an excused absence. Late work approvals must clearly link to an excused absence request per Student Rule 7.
Late assignments cannot be accepted for credit, without a valid reason.
If you feel that you have a valid reason for submitting late work, even if it is not related to Student Rule 7, submit a request to excuse the late submission (via a link to be provided) in advance of the deadline (3 business days in advance is required, except in an emergency). We will carefully review your request and will either approve or disapprove the request (with fairness and equity in mind) if the request has been submitted in a timely manner.

chunks.title: Course Schedule Overview (Subject to Change)
chunks.content:
Weeks 1-4: Project Framing and Early Design
Students are introduced to senior capstone expectations and industry-sponsored and faculty-proposed projects. Activities focus on identifying and formulating a computing problem, understanding domain context and prior work, functioning effectively in teams, ethical considerations, defining project goals, requirements, and success criteria, and establishing project management foundations. Assessment emphasizes iterative progress on early design, planning, and communication deliverables.

Weeks 5-7: Design Exploration and Evaluation Planning
Teams develop system designs derived from defined requirements and constraints. Students learn to create evaluation plans aligned with their definition of success and prepare for implementation through targeted skill development. Assessment emphasizes progress on design, documentation, planning, and evaluation preparation.

Weeks 8-11: Implementation and Evaluation
Teams implement their systems and continue iterative evaluation, including testing and review. User interface design and technical design decisions are communicated and refined. User evaluation planning and execution begin during this phase. Assessment emphasizes implementation progress, evaluation activities, and technical documentation.

Weeks 12-14: System Refinement and Presentation Preparation
Teams complete implementation and evaluation, analyze results, and determine implications. Students prepare and deliver in-class and external presentations and demonstrations. Assessment emphasizes system quality, evaluation results, communication, and professionalism.

Week 15: Final Deliverables and Review
Teams finalize all project deliverables and participate in final presentations, demonstrations, and reviews with industry or instructional staff. Final assessment considers project completion, documentation, evaluation results, and professional communication.

chunks.title: Course Expectations
chunks.content:
Course expectations, in the form of a course appreciative agreement, will be reviewed and agreed to in the first weeks of class.
The course provides direct instruction in writing and public speaking through lectures, workshops, peer review, assignment models, and opportunities for practice and revision. Formative feedback will be given on both writing and speaking assignments prior to final grading, and students are expected to revise and improve major submissions and presentations in response to instructor and peer evaluations.

chunks.title: Course Copyright
chunks.content: The materials used within this course are copyrighted and include, but are not limited to, the syllabi, quizzes, exams, homework, online handouts, course videos, audio, and visual recordings of classes, etc. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or distribute these materials, unless permission is explicitly granted.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
Writing: Use of Al tools, including ChatGPT, is permitted in this course for students who wish to use them. To adhere to our scholarly values, students must cite any Al-generated material that informed their work (this includes in-text citations and/or use of quotations, and in your reference list). Using an Al tool to generate content without proper attribution qualifies as academic dishonesty. See https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html fo r information how to properly cite Al tools. (adapted from University of Massachusetts Amherst, https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-97f1-4065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMUCTE GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations.pdf)
Computer Code. In principle you may submit Al-generated code, or code that is based on or derived from Al-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments: you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the response. Al tools may help you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing. Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.(from Boris Steipe (2023) “Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project http://sentientsyllabus.org .)

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
Attendance is important. Not attending weekly class meetings harms the other members of your group and makes it much more difficult for the instructor to assess your contributions to the group effort. Due to the undue burden your frequent absences have on the rest of the team, more than five unexcused absences are an automatic failure.
The instructors will evaluate your attendance through frequent quizzes, meetings, participation in the discussions, participation in assignments, and/or contributions to the team. Upon instructors' discretion, arriving to class late (> 5 minutes late), leaving early (> 5 minutes before class end), and/or not participating (e.g., not taking a quiz, not being present for a status meeting, not submitting in class work, and/or doing work not related to the class) can be considered an unexcused absence, depending on the circumstances. Oversleeping, a term paper due, an exam to cram for, etc., are not valid reasons. The instructor reserves the right to determine what constitutes a "valid reason” on a case-by-case basis.
Absence / lateness during days in which teams may need to work in a remote location to accomplish certain assignments (e.g., presenting your work outside of class, interviewing experts, meeting with mentors, accessing technologies outside of the classroom, etc.) count toward the 5 unexcused absences, unless the absence / lateness are approved by the instructor in advance. Students are expected to meet instructor's requirements for check in, activity reporting requirements, and/or timely submission of-class assignments.
You are responsible for monitoring / tracking your unexcused absences.
Students experiencing personal injury or illness that is too severe for the student to attend class qualify for an excused absence (see Student Rule 7, Section 7.2.2) assuming that documentationand notification guidelines are followed.
